title: Author tests for unauthorized read in blog API

ground_truth_issues.description: A test asserts that a non-owner GET /posts/{id} for a draft post owned by another user returns 403 Forbidden (or similar 4xx), not 200 with the draft content.
A test asserts that a non-owner GET /posts/{id} for any post (even published) owned by another user does not expose the author_id or internal metadata of the actual owner.
A test asserts that GET /posts (list endpoint) does not include draft posts from other authors in the response, or filters them by owner.
A test asserts that a non-owner PUT /posts/{id} to modify another user's post returns 403 Forbidden (or similar 4xx), not 200.
A test asserts that a non-owner DELETE /posts/{id} returns 403 Forbidden (or similar 4xx), not 200, and the post remains in the database.
An anti-overblock test asserts that the owner of a draft post CAN still GET that exact post, returning 200 and matching expected core fields (id, author_id, content/is_draft) for the owner context.
An anti-overblock test asserts that the owner of a post CAN still PUT (update) their own post, returning 200, and verifies the new values persist on a follow-up owner read/list check.
An anti-overblock test asserts that the owner of a post CAN still DELETE their own post, returning 200, and verifies the post is no longer retrievable/listed for that owner afterward.
An anti-overblock test asserts that GET /posts remains usable for a legitimate user, returning 200 and a list that includes that user's posts while excluding known non-owned draft fixtures.
A test asserts that accessing a non-existent post (GET /posts/999999) returns 404, not 403 or 500, for all users.
Tests use unique sentinel markers and assert those markers do not appear in cross-user list/detail responses, while still being observable by the rightful owner in owner-context assertions.
